ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific BBQ/grilled squid subclass exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers squids and cuttlefishes as a category; no BBQ-specific preparation subcode exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for BBQ squid as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of BBQ squid in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising one or two whole squid or several squid rings/tubes grilled and served with dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy cooked squid values used as the closest approved-source analogue for BBQ squid. BBQ preparation (dry-heat grilling with marinade) produces a nutrient profile similar to moist-heat cooked squid for core macros; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical soy-sauce-based marinade used in Singaporean BBQ squid preparations. Values represent the edible squid portion per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for BBQ squid. Squid is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood. The carbohydrate contribution comes primarily from the marinade (soy sauce, sugar). A GI of 40 is estimated by analogy with mixed protein-dominant seafood dishes with small amounts of added sugar/soy sauce, consistent with the low-GI range assigned to protein-rich seafood preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 7.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 6.24, rounded to 6.2.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving of BBQ squid provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing about 42% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: BBQ squid has a low estimated GI of 40 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.2 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich seafood choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided the marinade sugar content is kept moderate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content and sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (approximately 233 mg per 100 g) and the soy-sauce marinade adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency, while benefiting from the low saturated fat and omega-3 polyunsaturated fat content of squid.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Squid (烧烤鱿鱼; Sotong Bakar) is a popular grilled seafood dish served widely across Singapore and Southeast Asia, particularly in Chinese seafood restaurants and hawker-style settings. Fresh whole squid or squid tubes are marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, garlic, sesame oil, chilli, and sugar, then grilled or barbecued over charcoal or a gas flame until lightly charred and cooked through. The result is a tender, slightly smoky, savoury-sweet seafood with a firm yet yielding texture and a caramelised exterior. In Singapore, BBQ squid is commonly served as a sharing dish alongside steamed rice or other seafood items. Nutritionally, squid is a lean, high-protein seafood with low saturated fat, moderate cholesterol, and notable levels of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The BBQ preparation adds carbohydrates and sodium from the marinade. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (~40) due to its dominant protein content, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the sodium content from soy-sauce-based marinades warrants attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free, though it contains mollusc and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in BBQ squid?
Is grilled squid good for diabetics?
Can I eat BBQ squid if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for BBQ squid was available. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (NDB 15150) with sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical soy-sauce marinade. GI is an analogous estimate based on the protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate profile of squid with reference to the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted nutrient source and estimated GI.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry used as the nutrient source. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked squid — naturally zero for this seafood product. Dietary fibre (dietary_fibre_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked squid — naturally zero as squid is an animal-derived food with no plant cell-wall material. Sodium value of 480 mg per 100 g is an upward adaptation from the USDA baseline (~260 mg) to reflect typical soy-sauce-based BBQ marinade used in Singaporean preparations; this is a conservative estimate. The input cuisine_tag 'Crab' appears to be a platform tagging artefact and has not been carried forward as it does not describe this dish. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for BBQ squid was located in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases consistency — all aliases_by_language entries confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases array. Confirmed zero-macro justification phrases present in notes. Confirmed sentinel -1 documentation present. Confirmed GI threshold compliance. Self-scored 8/8 with all hard gates passing. Finalised as PASS.
